---
# Body only — paper-level metadata should be added to ../../_shared/papers.toml
# when this draft moves from outline to series metadata.
bibliography:
  - ../../_shared/references.bib
format:
  pdf:
    output-file: "RTR-WP-008-body.pdf"
    documentclass: article
    classoption: [11pt, a4paper]
    geometry: [top=20mm, bottom=20mm, left=28mm, right=28mm]
    pdf-engine: lualatex
    number-sections: true
    toc: true
    toc-title: "Contents"
    toc-depth: 2
    colorlinks: false
    linkcolor: "black"
    urlcolor: "black"
    citecolor: "black"
    include-in-header: ../../_shared/body-preamble.tex
    header-includes: |
      \usepackage{tabularx}
      \usepackage{xurl}
      \usepackage{setspace}
      \onehalfspacing
      \usepackage{float}
      \floatplacement{figure}{!htbp}
      \floatplacement{table}{!htbp}
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
  message: false
---

# From Reader to User

## Eighty Years of the Indian Library Association and the Changing Public of the Library, 1942-2019

### Draft Status

This is a working-paper scaffold. The evidentiary claims below must be revised
after the OCR run over the Indian Library Association proceedings is complete,
segmented, and sampled for OCR quality. The ILA corpus currently consists of 40
downloaded proceedings records from the NDLI Indian Library Association DSpace
collection, spanning 1942-2019.

# Abstract

This paper asks how the Indian Library Association's discourse transformed the
imagined purpose, public, and institutional form of the library between 1942
and 2019. It treats 40 ILA conference proceedings as a professional archive of
Indian library thought and reads them beside adjacent historical and policy
records: Baroda's public-library system, Ranganathan's library-movement
writings, the Advisory Committee for Libraries, the Planning Commission working
group, state public-library bills and review committees, and the National
Knowledge Commission. The paper is part of a broader historical-sociological
inquiry into the changing place of libraries in Indian society. Within that
larger project, this corpus analysis supplies the professional-discourse
record: it shows how librarians and library institutions made the library
legible across successive languages of civic formation, planning, public
infrastructure, information systems, management, knowledge society, and digital
services. The digital-humanities layer of the study downloads and preserves
the ILA corpus, runs OCR and text extraction, audits OCR quality, and produces
descriptive NLP evidence. The critical-interpretive layer asks what social
publics become visible or disappear as the library moves from reader/citizen
toward user/information seeker.

# Core Claim

Across the ILA proceedings, the library appears first as a civic-democratic and
educational institution, then as a planned-development infrastructure, then as
an information/documentation system, and finally as a digital and
service-management platform. The paper asks what is gained in professional and
technological sophistication, and what is lost when the "reader" or "citizen"
becomes the "user" or "information seeker."

The ILA proceedings should be treated as the spine of the paper. Other Zotero
records enter as counter-records: moments where professional speech meets
state policy, princely or regional public-library experiments, public-library
law, and later knowledge-economy or digital-library discourse.

# Introduction: Why Read ILA Discursively?

This paper asks how the Indian Library Association's discourse transformed the
imagined purpose, public, and institutional form of the library between 1942
and 2019.

It opens from a simple proposition: ILA conference proceedings are not neutral
professional literature. They are institutional speech. They show what Indian
librarians, library educators, administrators, and associated policy actors
made thinkable, urgent, technical, political, or unspeakable at different
historical moments.

The research question:

How did the Indian library profession describe the purpose of libraries across
colonial crisis, national planning, public-library legislation, information
policy, liberalization, digitization, and Digital India?

The introduction should establish three claims.

First, the ILA corpus lets us read the profession's own changing vocabulary:
`reader`, `public`, `citizen`, `adult education`, `social education`,
`information`, `user`, `quality`, `knowledge society`, `digital`, and
`sustainability`.

Second, the corpus must be read beside state and regional records. The ILA
does not speak in a vacuum; it responds to advisory committees, planning
language, state library acts, public-library bills, and later national
knowledge-economy frameworks.

Third, the right-to-read question is not only whether libraries existed, but
what kind of public the profession imagined libraries to serve.

# Research Question

The paper asks: how did the Indian Library Association's discourse transform
the imagined purpose, public, and institutional form of the library between
1942 and 2019?

Three subsidiary questions organize the inquiry.

First, how does the ILA vocabulary shift from the language of readers, public
libraries, adult education, social education, finance, and legislation toward
the language of users, information services, networks, quality, management,
knowledge, and digital systems?

Second, how does ILA discourse relate to adjacent state and historical records:
the Baroda public-library system, the Advisory Committee for Libraries, the
Planning Commission working group, state public-library bills and review
committees, and the National Knowledge Commission?

Third, what publics become visible or disappear across this transition,
especially readers marked by caste, language, rurality, gender, disability,
library labour, and fiscal dependence on the state?

## Interleaved Records

- `Baroda and its libraries` (1928, Zotero `4QZK3GV7`) as a pre-ILA example
  of princely/state-supported public-library modernity.
- `Libraries and Library Movement` by S. R. Ranganathan (1941, Zotero
  `3I9WSMT5`) as immediate pre-history.
- `The Origin and Development of the Library Movement in the Bombay Presidency`
  (1947, Zotero `FGVD8X9K`) as a regional movement genealogy.

# Method: Mixed Methods and Digital Humanities

This is a mixed-methods study combining a reproducible digital-humanities
corpus pipeline with critical interpretation. The digital-humanities layer
creates the corpus, records provenance, extracts text, audits OCR quality, and
generates descriptive NLP evidence. The critical-interpretive layer reads the
verified passages, institutional vocabulary, silences, and historical shifts.

The source corpus was acquired from the Indian Library Association collection
hosted on the National Digital Library of India infrastructure. The collection
metadata and PDFs were downloaded from the ILA DSpace service, with provenance
captured in local manifests. The acquisition records include handle, source
URL, title, date issued, publisher, original PDF path, file size, and SHA-256
hash.

The PDFs were first inspected with standard PDF tools. `pdftotext` was used to
test whether text was natively extractable. The completed checks showed that
the ILA proceedings are scanned image PDFs rather than born-digital text PDFs,
including the recent volumes inspected in this run. The scanned PDFs were then
processed through a Python-controlled OCR workflow using OCRmyPDF and Tesseract
(`eng`) with page rotation and deskewing enabled. For each volume, the workflow
produces an OCR PDF, a sidecar text file, an OCR log, and a manifest row with
checksums and text length.

After OCR, Python scripts parse the sidecar text and generate descriptive
NLP artifacts: term counts by volume, normalized term drift by period,
concordance targets, and quality summaries. The current descriptive pass uses
terms such as `reader`, `user`, `citizen`, `public library`, `adult education`,
`social education`, `information`, `network`, `computerization`, `quality`,
`management`, `digital`, `knowledge`, `caste`, `language`, `finance`, and
`labour`. These counts are used for discovery and orientation, not as a
substitute for interpretation.

The corpus is OCR-derived, with page-level verification as the evidentiary
standard for claims. The first quality pass covers the 30 completed proceedings
from 1942-2008: none are graded `poor`, seventeen are graded
`usable-close-reading`, and thirteen are graded `usable-concordance`. The OCR
layer is used for discovery, term drift, and concordance; page verification
decides what can be quoted and argued from the source. The detailed audit is
kept at `data/ila-conference-proceedings/quality/ocr_quality_report.md`.


# Preliminary Term-Drift Findings, 1942-2008

The first non-interpretive pass supports the paper's periodization as a working
hypothesis. Normalized counts per 10,000 words show `user` rising from near
absence in the 1940s to 52.449 in the 2000-2008 subset. `information` rises
from 3.332 to 149.283 across the same span. `digital`, absent in the early
periods, reaches 37.649 in 2000-2008. `quality` and `management` also rise
sharply in the post-2000 proceedings.

The same table shows a planning-era peak and later decline in explicitly
public-library and fiscal language. `public_library` rises to 49.594 in
1950-1969, then falls to 7.299 in 2000-2008. `finance` rises to 39.124 in
1950-1969, then falls to 8.156 in 2000-2008. `adult_education` peaks in
1970-1985 and is nearly absent in 2000-2008.

These are discovery counts, not final claims. They identify where close reading
should begin: the shift from `reader` to `user`, from `public library` and
`finance` to `information`, `quality`, `management`, `digital`, and
`knowledge`, and the persistent thinness of caste-explicit vocabulary. The
underlying artifacts are:

- `data/ila-conference-proceedings/analysis/term_drift_summary_1942_2008.md`
- `data/ila-conference-proceedings/analysis/term_drift_by_period_1942_2008.csv`
- `data/ila-conference-proceedings/analysis/term_counts_by_volume_1942_2008.csv`

# Before the Republic: Library as Civic Formation

Period: Baroda 1928, Ranganathan 1941, ILA 1942 and 1944.

Question:

Before development planning, what was a library imagined to do?

Likely themes to test in the corpus:

- public reading;
- adult education;
- reference books;
- classification;
- professional training;
- rural reconstruction;
- library as civic discipline.

The Baroda report should not be treated as a quaint prelude. It is a record of
library modernity before the postcolonial planning state. It can show how a
state-supported library system was imagined before the ILA's own institutional
language stabilized.

The Mysore/Tipu material in Zotero should be used cautiously. The currently
identified Mysore-adjacent record is `Plunder and Prestige: Tipu Sultan's
Library and the Making of British India` (Zotero `MYJ5E24F`). That belongs as
background on library, sovereignty, and colonial capture, not as evidence for
twentieth-century public-library policy unless a more direct Mysore
public-library report is added.

# The Planning State: Library as Public Infrastructure

Period: 1950s-1960s.

Question:

How did the library become a planned institution of the developmental state?

ILA nodes:

- 1962, 1964, and 1965 proceedings;
- library legislation;
- state library systems;
- Fourth Five Year Plan language;
- social education;
- librarian status, pay, training, and service conditions.

Interleaved records:

- `Report of Advisory Committee for libraries` (GIPE, 1959, Zotero
  `85IVM7VW`).
- `Report of the Advisory Committee for Libraries` (Internet Archive/Central
  Secretariat Library, 1961, Zotero `PQFDDGJY`) as a duplicate-review/source
  variant of the Advisory Committee report, not a separate committee report
  unless later title-page evidence establishes that distinction.
- `Report of the working group on libraries` (Planning Commission, 1966,
  Zotero `TCWLEJSW`).

Analytical move:

Compare ILA's professional demands with state committee language. Does ILA ask
for rights and infrastructure, or for recognition, status, administration, and
professional jurisdiction? Where do those demands overlap, and where do they
diverge?

## The Delhi Public Library: a model for Asia

The planning state's library vision had a flagship, and it was built for
display. The Delhi Public Library was established in 1951 as a joint Government
of India--UNESCO pilot project --- one of two in the world, the other in
Colombia --- and consecrated across 1956--57, when UNESCO published the proceedings of the
"Public Libraries for Asia" seminar it had hosted there (1956) and its evaluation
report of the library (Public Library Manuals, Vol. 8, 1957). Its stated purpose was to "adapt modern techniques to Indian
conditions" and to serve as "a model public library for Asia" [@stdcomm2022culture]. By 1955 UNESCO could call it "the busiest and most
modern public library in Asia" and use it as "the headquarters and the
'laboratory'" for a seminar drawing delegates from twelve Asian countries
[@PublicLibrariesAsia2024].

The institution belongs to the founding wave of the republic: the Constitution
in force in 1950, the Planning Commission in 1950, the First Plan and the Delhi
Public Library in 1951. It was the work of the Ministry of Education under
Maulana Abul Kalam Azad --- the same ministry that would convene the Advisory
Committee for Libraries in 1957 --- with Nehru as its symbolic apex; his
ninety-minute visit to the 1955 seminar, filmed for newsreels across India, was
staged as the project's benediction. The library expressed a Nehruvian
self-image: non-aligned, developmental, and addressed to Asia. To read it as
merely a building is to miss that the same postwar state had already made Delhi
the convening-capital of anticolonial Asia at the 1947 Asian Relations
Conference. The library-as-"model for Asia" is one node in a wider
knowledge-infrastructure imaginary --- conferences, institutes, area-studies
schemes, state publication --- through which the new state staged Delhi as a
capital of Asian knowledge [@elhalabyPartingGiftsEmpire2025]. This is a relation of conditions of
possibility, not cause: the conference did not produce the library; the same
state produced both.

The library's universalism is worth reading closely, because it is euphemistic.
In his inaugural address Azad located India's backwardness in the history of
restricted reading: great libraries had existed, but "the benefits of these
libraries were confined to royalty and the nobility ... knowledge was not
widespread among the masses. One main reason why India fell behind Europe is to
be found in this restriction of libraries for the use of only a selected few."
"Today," he concluded, "democratic India ... is providing facilities for
education and enlightenment for all her children" [@PublicLibrariesAsia2024]. The diagnosis
is half-right and tellingly coded: the monopoly on letters is named as feudal
--- "royalty and nobility," "a selected few" --- never as caste. The remedy is
an undifferentiated "all her children," a reading citizen whose caste the state
does not name.

That silence has a face. The most consequential reader of the era, B. R.
Ambedkar --- who held that every person should spend a tenth of their income on
books, and who assembled a personal library of some fifty thousand volumes at
Rajgriha because the public and Brahmanical order of letters had been built to
exclude him --- resigned from Nehru's cabinet in 1951, the very year the Delhi
Public Library opened its universal doors. The corpus records no comment from him
on the library itself. He is the absent reader at its founding: the figure who
made the caste monopoly on the written word the centre of his politics, leaving
the government as it inaugurated a "library for all" that would not say the word.
The Delhi Public Library's universalism and Ambedkar's exit are the two halves
of the planning state's relation to the reader --- the promise, and the unnamed
exclusion it is built upon.

<!-- CITATIONS TO WIRE (this subsection): Standing Committee on Culture #310 2022
  = Zotero FDGNQ8CH; Gardner, Public Libraries for Asia: the Delhi Seminar, UNESCO
  1956 = GBH3RILT; The Delhi Public Library: an evaluation report, UNESCO 1956 =
  VNLGGQQQ; Elhalaby, Parting Gifts of Empire 2025 = YVNSIG5M; Ambedkar 50k-book
  Rajgriha library = Rajgruha NABU4AQX; 10%-on-books + bibliophile = Liang & Gupta
  3J37J5WR p.7-8. Verify Ambedkar 1951 cabinet-resignation date against a primary
  source before final. -->

# Federalization and Law: Library as State Obligation

Period: late 1960s-1970s, with retrospective reference to later legal
scholarship.

Question:

What happens when public-library discourse moves into state legislation and
committees?

ILA nodes:

- 1979, 1980, 1982, and 1985 proceedings;
- state systems;
- public-library acts;
- finance;
- adult education;
- national information system.

Interleaved records:

- Andhra review committee report on the Andhra Pradesh Public Libraries Act,
  1960 (Zotero `UQU84SFW`).
- Gujarat Public Libraries Bill select committee report (Zotero `RIYYM3ST`).
- Kerala Public Libraries Bill select committee report (Zotero `BV6RETS6`).
- `Public Library Legislation in the New Millennium` (Zotero `N8AAA4WU`) as a
  later retrospective legal comparison.

Analytical move:

Ask whether "public library" becomes a state obligation or remains a
professional aspiration. Track the movement between law, finance, professional
standards, and public entitlement.

# The Information Turn: Library as System, Network, Database

Period: 1980s-1990s.

ILA nodes:

- 1982: public libraries and a national information system;
- 1985: national policy for library and information services;
- 1990: computerization and library network;
- 1991: national information policies and programmes;
- 1992: library and information technology;
- 1994-1999: library movement, twenty-first century preparation, electronic
  information era, and Library Vision 2010.

Question:

When "information" enters, what happens to "reading"?

Codes:

- information system;
- database;
- network;
- resource sharing;
- documentation;
- excellence;
- quality;
- service.

Analytical move:

This is likely the hinge chapter. The library is no longer only a place for
readers and books; it becomes a node in information infrastructure. The
chapter should test whether this expansion strengthens the public-library
mission or displaces it into professional and technical language.

# 1989-1991: The Library Loses Its Commons

The information turn is usually read as a technological event: the arrival of
the computer, the database, the network. Read against the term-drift evidence,
it is also a conjunctural one. Across the full 1942-2019 series (all forty
proceedings, 9.3 million words), the profession's vocabulary breaks hardest in
the blocks bracketing 1991 --- `user` rising from 13.0 (1970-85) to 34.8
(1986-99) to 47.1 (2000-08) per 10,000 words, `information` from 69 to 114 to
136, while `public_library` falls from its planning-era peak of 26.8 (1950-69)
to 8.1 (1986-99) and then 4.1 (2000-08). That break straddles three events that
arrive together: the fall of the Berlin Wall in 1989 and the dissolution of the Soviet
Union in 1991; the Indian balance-of-payments crisis and the liberalisation of
1991; and the first public reach of the internet across the decade that
followed. For the library these are not three events but one. The profession's
own language registers the moment the library stopped being imagined as a
commons and began to be imagined as a terminal.

What was the commons that was lost? It had two registers, neither of them the
state's flagship institution --- the Delhi Public Library, founded in 1951 as
the first UNESCO Public Library Pilot Project, was a modernisation instrument, a
"model public library for Asia." The first register was the planning state's own
vision of public-library provision as a public obligation. The Advisory
Committee for Libraries of 1957, chaired by K.P. Sinha, the first
post-independence report to treat public-library development extensively, framed
the library through social and adult education and laid out a finance plan
running from the Third to the Seventh Plan. The Working Group on Development of
Libraries during the Fourth Five Year Plan (1966-71) went further: it held that
the Central and State Governments "should share among them the entire
responsibility of providing adequate public library services to the citizens,"
recommended a Directorate of Libraries in every State, a minimum of Rs 30.99
crore for public libraries and Rs 20 crore for book production over the Plan
period, and prepared a draft Model Library Bill to bind the obligation in law.
This was the library as a public claim on knowledge, stated by the developmental
state in its own words.

The second register was collective rather than statal. It lived in the reading
rooms. In Kerala the *granthasala* and *vayanasala* were the organising cells of
the communist movement --- "where there is a local party office there is a
library" --- and the library was less a repository than an "energizing script of
self-improvement," a site where a collective political subject was made
[@thiranagama2026monster]. That subject had a material base. The cheap Soviet book ---
Progress and Mir imprints, moved through the state export monopoly
Mezhdunarodnaya Kniga and distributed in India through a network of Communist
Party book houses (People's Publishing House, Visalaandhra, Navakarnataka,
Prabhath), from a central node at the USSR Book Centre on Connaught Circus in
Delhi --- made a left reading economy viable at prices Indian commercial
editions could not match. Reading room and subsidised book were two halves of
one infrastructure of collective reading.

After 1991 both registers lost their backbone at once. The rouble-rupee book
trade collapsed with the state that subsidised it, and liberalisation
re-commercialised the Indian book market in the same years; the state's own
provisioning ambition, never fully realised, was quietly abandoned rather than
renewed. With the socialist alternative gone, "access to knowledge" was free to
be recoded --- from a collective good secured by public provision into a service
delivered to an individual through a network. This is the deeper content of the
reader-to-user shift: the *user* is the reader stripped of the collective. One
reads in a commons; one queries a system alone.

The internet's rise did not merely add a tool to this settlement; it occupied
the conceptual space the public-library commons had vacated. The promise that
information could be made costlessly available over a wire arrived precisely as
the institution embodying the public claim on knowledge was being defunded and
re-described. The recoding is legible in the state's own reports, not only the
profession's: where the 1966 Working Group spoke of a responsibility to the
citizen, the Eighth Plan Working Group on Libraries and *Informatics* (1990-95)
and the Planning Commission's Core Task Group on the Sharing of Library and
Information *Technology* renamed the same object as a problem of systems and
connectivity. Hence the misidentification that still organises Indian knowledge
policy: that digital connectivity *is* library provision --- that the network
lets a poor but literate public leapfrog the slow work of physical, fiscal,
staffed, vernacular reading infrastructure. The proceedings of the 2000s and 2010s, where
`digital` climbs from near-absence to 28.7 (2000-08) and then 41.7 (2009-19) ---
the one term still rising at the end of the series --- while `public_library`
(4.1) and `finance` (0.6) reach their lowest recorded levels, are the
professional voice of that substitution.

The loss is doubled by caste, and here the section must refuse nostalgia. The
commons that 1991 dissolved was never the open commons it claimed to be. The
reading room was proposed as a caste-free associational space but reconstituted
caste rather than abolishing it, building a "national popular" reading public
that consistently occluded Dalit participation [@thiranagama2026monster]; the planning
state's provisioning vision, for its part, addressed an abstract "citizen" whose
caste it did not name. The displacement of the library by the network is
therefore not the fall of an achieved equality. It is the substitution of one
caste-occluding form of access --- the collective that assumed caste away ---
for another --- the individualised terminal that assumes connectivity away. The
constitutional reader for whom reading is an unfinished anti-caste claim is
absent from both. In the term-drift series `caste` never exceeds 0.45 per 10,000
words --- across all forty volumes and 9.3 million words --- and stands at 0.23
in the most recent (2009-2019) block. That near-silence, running across the whole
1942-2019 record and straight through the rupture of 1991, is itself the finding
this paper is built to read.

<!-- CITATIONS TO WIRE INTO ../../_shared/references.bib before render:
  Standing Committee on Culture #310 (DPL 1951 UNESCO pilot) = Zotero FDGNQ8CH
  CSL Compendium of Govt Reports on Library Services 2003 (Sinha 1957; Fourth
    Plan Working Group; Informatics WGs) = Zotero J886QGZU
  Thiranagama, Rural Civilities 2022 = XPSCQSXK; The Monster in Your Path 2026 = 4IRZGFG6
  Laugesen, UNESCO and the Globalization of the Public Library Idea = ZPU2F59I
  Books from USSR, Soviet Review 1981 = IA books-from-ussr-soviet-review-1981
  Term-drift figures: data/ila-conference-proceedings/analysis/term_drift_by_period_1942_2008.csv
  ILA close-reading passages (2000s proceedings) still owed per verification protocol. -->

# Liberalization and Management: Library as Service Organization

Period: 2001-2010.

ILA nodes:

- 2001: `Quest for Quality`;
- 2001: `Library Practices for Effective Management`;
- 2003: `Electronic Information Environment`;
- 2004: `Responding to Users' Need`;
- 2004: `Knowledge Organization in Digital Environment`;
- 2005: `Libraries, Information Literacy and Lifelong Learning`;
- 2006: `Information Commons`;
- subsequent 2007-2010 proceedings after OCR completion.

Question:

Who replaces the reader? The "user."

Trace:

- quality;
- management;
- effective service;
- changing information landscape;
- information literacy;
- lifelong learning;
- information commons.

Interleaved record:

- National Knowledge Commission, `Report of the Working Group on Libraries`
  (2006, Zotero `TLKKE95B`).

Analytical move:

Use the National Knowledge Commission as the state/elite knowledge-economy
rearticulation of the library question. The chapter should ask whether
`information literacy` and `lifelong learning` extend the older social/adult
education discourse or convert it into individualized skills and user-service
language.

# Digital India and the Platform Library

Period: 2011-2019.

ILA nodes:

- next generation libraries;
- knowledge society;
- embedded librarianship;
- technological challenges;
- Digital India;
- sustainable development of the LIS profession;
- transforming libraries and information centres in the digital era.

Question:

Does digital access renew the public-library mission, or does it dissolve it
into ICT and service language?

Interleaved records:

- Andhra Pradesh digital-library records in Zotero;
- Gujarat library expansion and digital-modernization news records in Zotero;
- `Public Libraries and Open Government`;
- `National Information and Library Policies in Support of the UN Sustainable
  Development Goals` (Zotero `6SJDCY3I`);
- contemporary public-library legislation scholarship where relevant.

Analytical move:

Separate "digital access for publics" from "digital modernization of
institutions." They are not the same thing. The key test is whether the public
returns as reader/citizen/community, or appears only as user, beneficiary, or
data subject.

# The Silence Ledger

This should be a standalone chapter, not a footnote.

Ask across all periods:

- Where are caste and Bahujan readers?
- Where are vernacular publics?
- Where are rural women, children, disabled readers?
- Where is library labour?
- Where is finance?
- Where is the constitutional citizen?
- When `user` appears, what social body disappears?

The chapter should use both absence and euphemism. Terms such as `public`,
`community`, `user`, `people`, `rural`, `backward`, and `weaker sections` may
do political work without naming caste, class, language, disability, or labour.

# What the Archive Shows: Refusal, the Dissolved Reader, and the Caste-Silence

The preceding chapters trace a vocabulary. This one reads what the vocabulary is
the symptom of. Three independent record-sets --- the ILA proceedings, the
government library reports compiled in the Central Secretariat Library's 2003
*Compendium of Select Government Reports on Library and Information Services in
India*, and the post-1990 parliamentary record --- turn out to be depositions of
a single discursive formation: one that constitutes the Indian reading public as
a caste-blind universal whose coherence depends on caste remaining unsayable, and
whose material correlate is a costed-then-refused provision. Four operations hold
that formation together.

## Refusal by deferral

The state did not neglect the public library; it studied it, repeatedly, for
forty-five years, and the studying was the form the neglect took. The *Compendium*
assembles the chain: the Advisory Committee for Libraries (Sinha, 1957); the
Fourth Plan Working Group (1966-71); the Seventh Plan Working Group on
Modernisation (1985-90); the Committee on a National Policy on Library and
Information System (NAPLIS, 1986) and its Empowered Committee (1988); the
follow-up Working Group (1992); the Eighth Plan Working Group on Libraries and
Informatics (1990-95); the Core Task Group chaired by A.P.J. Abdul Kalam (1995);
and the Ninth Plan Working Group (1997-2002). Across this chain the same four
structural reforms are recommended and re-recommended: a binding national policy
or legislation; a National Commission on Library and Information System; the
transfer of public libraries from the State List to the Concurrent List so the
Centre could legislate universal provision; and the explicit extension of service
to the "weaker sections," "backward areas," and "neo-literates." None of the four
was enacted. No national library law; no National Commission; public libraries
remained a State subject; provision decayed to the per-capita floor the
measurement paper documents. The repetition is not a failure within the discourse
but the discourse's function: each committee re-discovers the identical problem,
re-recommends the identical fix, the fix is not enacted, and a new committee is
constituted to re-study it. The committee chain is the institutional form of the
refusal --- the state performing perpetual concern as the technique of withholding
provision. Two artefacts in the chain are decisive. First, the state's own
admission of elite capture: the 1995 Core Task Group recorded that the country's
information infrastructure had "rather low" impact because "the services are not
effective due to limiting its users only to scientists in elite institutions" ---
and answered that diagnosis with more networks and digitisation, not universal
provision. Second, the "State subject" deflection is exposed as a *declined
reform*: the Eighth Plan Working Group recommended moving public libraries to the
Concurrent List precisely so universal provision could be legislated; the
recommendation was refused; and every subsequent "libraries are a State subject"
answer cites as a constitutional given what was in fact a reform the state chose
not to make.

## The dissolution of the reader into a function

Read archaeologically rather than lexically, the reader-to-user shift is not a
change of words but a change in what the library constitutes as its subject.
Across the proceedings the library is successively an instrument of civic
formation (the reader as a citizen-in-the-making), then planned infrastructure
(the citizen with a claim), then a node in an information system (the user as a
function of that system), then a digital access-point (the user as beneficiary or
data-subject). The movement is from a *person* --- to be emancipated, who holds a
claim --- to a *function* --- a query, a footfall, a download. This is why the
term-drift matters beyond its arithmetic: the dissolution of the reading person
into the system-function is precisely what renders the caste question unaskable.
One can ask of a person, but not of a function, who by caste is permitted to read.
The discourse protects itself not by denying caste but by dissolving the subject
of whom the caste question could be asked.

## The constitutive silence

The flat near-absence of caste across the series --- never above 0.45 per 10,000
words, across forty volumes and 9.3 million words --- is not a gap but a
condition. The discourse can constitute its universal reader only on condition
that caste remains unspeakable; to name caste would be to concede that the reading
subject is caste-structured and the universal false. The silence is what makes the
universal coherent. The decisive artefact is Gore's 1955 survey of the Delhi
Public Library: a full apparatus of measurement --- sex, age, occupation,
education, distance --- with no category for caste. The instrument was built
unable to see caste. Azad's inaugural address performs the same operation in
speech: it sees the historical monopoly on letters ("the restriction of libraries
for the use of only a selected few") but can name it only as feudal, "royalty and
nobility," before dissolving it into "all her children." The unsayable thing is
the true thing.

## The three publics, and the one that was erased

The silence has a history. Across the twentieth century the Indian reading public
was constituted three times over. The nationalist library movement --- fused to
the freedom struggle, its association meeting alongside sessions of the Indian
National Congress --- imagined a universal "common man" who was caste-blind. The
communist library --- the Kerala *granthasala*, the study circle --- made reading
an act of political consciousness but built what its own historians call a
"national popular" that "consistently occluded Dalits' participation." And the
anti-caste tradition --- Phule's schools, Periyar's and Sahodaran Ayyappan's
reading rooms, the Travancore movement, Ambedkar --- constituted reading as
specifically an anti-caste act: the Bahujan reader, caste-explicit. Of the three,
only the anti-caste tradition ever named caste. The national library settlement
universalised the nationalist reader, absorbed the communist energy into "social
education," and never institutionalised the anti-caste reader at all. The
caste-silence of the professional and state discourse is therefore not an
oversight but the trace of a won contest: the caste-blind reading public defeated
the caste-explicit one at the level of the state, and the silence is what that
victory sounds like.

## The regional reading-movements

The "library as people's movement" was never a single national movement; it was
a constellation of regional reading-movements, and it is in the regions that the
contest between the three publics is documented. The Proceedings of the Indian
History Congress preserve two of them: Priolkar's account of the Bombay
Presidency movement [@priolkar1947origin] and, more sharply, G. Samba Siva
Reddy's study of the reading rooms of Rayalaseema, 1858--1947, whose very title
--- "Creating Consciousness Among the Colonised" --- names reading as a political
act [@reddy2009consciousness]. The Telugu country is the densest case. The Andhra
library movement, organised from 1914 through the *Grandhalaya Sangham* under
Ayyanki Venkata Ramanayya (1890--1979) [@venkatappaiah2006legislation], built the
institutional substrate of the regional public sphere that Rama Sundari Mantena
reconstructs --- a "public politics based on liberal ideas of public reason and
debate" across the Telugu districts of Madras and princely Hyderabad
[@mantenaAndhraMovementHyderabad2014]. That same Andhra Mahasabha and its reading
publics carried, at their radical edge, into the Telangana armed struggle of
1946--51 [@sundarayyaTelanganaPeoplesStruggle1972] --- reading as revolutionary
consciousness, the Andhra counterpart to the Kerala *granthasala*
[@thiranagama2022rural] and the Tamil Arivoli Iyakkam
[@codyLightKnowledgeLiteracy2013]. These vernacular libraries were, as Boyk,
Amstutz and Perkins argue, "constructed and maintained beyond the ambit of the
colonial or post-colonial states" yet "deeply enmeshed in varied state projects"
[@boyk2020unpacking]. The national settlement did not build on this regional
energy; it absorbed it. After 1960 the regional reading-movement was
professionalised into "social education" and "public library legislation," and
its political and anti-caste charge drained out. The question the regional record
forces is the one the national discourse cannot ask: when Reddy's reading rooms
created consciousness "among the colonised," and Mantena's public sphere convened
the Telugu "public," whose consciousness, and which public --- and was the Dalit
reader inside it, or its constitutive outside?

## A comparative control

The silence is specific, not generic. In the 1996 *Daedalus* issue "Books, Bricks,
and Bytes" --- a single volume in which the postwar library establishment
reframed itself around the digital transition --- the postcolonial chapters name
their societies' stratification: the South African chapter names race and
apartheid [@lor1996distant], the Brazilian chapter names social inequality in its very title
[@santanna1996inequality]. The Indian chapter [@banerjee1996story], narrating "the story of libraries
in India" in the same volume and under the same digital-future frame, does not
name caste. The comparison is the control: the silence is not a universal feature
of postcolonial library discourse, which elsewhere could say its inequality. It is
specific to the Indian caste case. What the other national stories could say, the
Indian one could not.

## What the archive shows

These are not three datasets and a comparison but four faces of one formation. The
state calculates the cost of a universal reading public and refuses it by
deferral; the profession dissolves the reading person into a system-function; both
produce a universal reader whose coherence requires caste's unsayability; and the
comparative record shows that this unsayability is the Indian case's signature,
not the genre's. A public library matters most to those who cannot buy books ---
disproportionately the caste-Bahujan poor --- so the refusal of universal public
provision is caste exclusion administered in the neutral languages of fiscal
priority, "State subject," and "digital access." The reports calculate what
including the Bahujan reader would cost; the formation's work is to ensure the
calculation never becomes a library, and the exclusion never becomes a sentence
with the word "caste" in it. To read that silence as the answer is the task this
paper sets itself.

<!-- CITATIONS TO WIRE (this section): CSL Compendium 2003 = Zotero J886QGZU
  (committee chain; Kalam Core Task Group elite-capture quote; Concurrent-List
  recommendation). Term-drift figures = analysis/term_drift_by_period_1942_2019.csv.
  Gore survey + Azad address = DPL Evaluation Report, UNESCO Public Library Manuals
  Vol. 8, 1957 (data/dpl-unesco). Three publics: Banerjee, "The Story of Libraries
  in India," Daedalus 125(4) 1996 = MLQCIREE; Venkatappaiah & Madhusudhan = N8AAA4WU;
  Thiranagama "Rural Civilities" = XPSCQSXK / "The Monster in Your Path" = 4IRZGFG6;
  Cody = PXQAHGJ9; anti-caste lineage per RTR preprint 4RLIRDP3. Comparative control:
  Lor (S. Africa), de Sant'Anna (Brazil, H2SWIQGZ), Banerjee (India) — Daedalus
  "Books, Bricks, and Bytes" 125(4) 1996 (Zotero collection 9BUD3JEJ). -->

# Conclusion: What the ILA Archive Shows

Possible closing argument:

The ILA archive shows a profession repeatedly trying to make libraries legible
to the dominant state language of each period: reconstruction, planning,
information policy, management, digitization. The cost is that the library's
democratic subject becomes unstable: reader, adult learner, citizen, student,
user, information seeker, knowledge worker.

That lets the paper end with a contemporary claim: the right to read cannot be
rebuilt only through digital-library language. It requires recovering the
library as public, fiscal, linguistic, and anti-caste infrastructure.

# Verification Protocol and Future Work

This draft begins from scanned proceedings. OCR supplies the discovery layer;
page-level verification supplies the evidentiary layer. The next version must
finish OCR for the full 1942-2019 collection, segment papers and addresses
where possible, and carry verified passages into the argument with page-level
citations.

# Evidence Tables To Generate

## Timeline of ILA Proceedings

Generate from:

`data/ila-conference-proceedings/manifest.jsonl`

Fields:

- year;
- title;
- handle;
- Zotero key;
- original PDF path;
- OCR PDF path;
- OCR text path;
- OCR character count;
- OCR warnings.

## Concordance Ledger

Generate by decade for:

- `reader`;
- `user`;
- `citizen`;
- `public`;
- `rural`;
- `adult education`;
- `social education`;
- `information`;
- `documentation`;
- `network`;
- `computerization`;
- `quality`;
- `management`;
- `digital`;
- `knowledge society`;
- `lifelong learning`;
- `information literacy`;
- `caste`;
- `scheduled caste`;
- `scheduled tribe`;
- `backward`;
- `vernacular`;
- `language`;
- `finance`;
- `cess`;
- `labour`;
- `staff`.

## Zotero Interleaving Table

Seed records:

| Role | Zotero key | Record |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Pre-ILA princely/state-supported public-library modernity | `4QZK3GV7` | `Baroda and its libraries` |
| Immediate pre-history | `3I9WSMT5` | `Libraries and Library Movement` |
| Regional movement genealogy | `FGVD8X9K` | `The Origin and Development of the Library Movement in the Bombay Presidency` |
| Planning-state advisory report | `85IVM7VW` | `Report of Advisory Committee for libraries` |
| Duplicate-review source variant | `PQFDDGJY` | `Report of the Advisory Committee for Libraries` |
| Planning Commission report | `TCWLEJSW` | `Report of the working group on libraries` |
| Andhra library-law review | `UQU84SFW` | Andhra Pradesh Public Libraries Act review committee report |
| Gujarat library-law committee | `RIYYM3ST` | Gujarat Public Libraries Bill select committee report |
| Kerala library-law committee | `BV6RETS6` | Kerala Public Libraries Bill select committee report |
| Later public-library legislation comparison | `N8AAA4WU` | `Public Library Legislation in the New Millennium` |
| Knowledge-economy state reframing | `TLKKE95B` | National Knowledge Commission `Report of the Working Group on Libraries` |
| Mysore/sovereignty background, not public-library-policy evidence | `MYJ5E24F` | `Plunder and Prestige: Tipu Sultan's Library and the Making of British India` |

## Duplicate and Source-Review Table

Start from:

`data/zotero-rights2reads-audit/duplicate_parent_titles.csv`

Minimum issue to carry into the paper:

- `85IVM7VW` and `PQFDDGJY` appear to be duplicate bibliographic records for
  the same Advisory Committee report, with different catalog sources/scans and
  different dates. Do not treat them as two distinct committee reports unless
  further title-page evidence proves that.

## OCR Quality Table

Generate from:

`data/ila-conference-proceedings/ocr_manifest.jsonl`

Minimum fields:

- year;
- title;
- pages if available from `pdfinfo`;
- OCR text characters;
- OCR engine;
- elapsed time;
- warning count from log;
- spot-check status;
- whether OCR is sufficient for concordance, close reading, or only metadata.

# Immediate Next Steps

1. Finish OCR for all 40 ILA PDFs.
2. Verify `ocr_manifest.jsonl` has 40 records and no missing OCR PDFs/texts.
3. Build the timeline, concordance ledger, and Zotero interleaving table.
4. Segment proceedings by table of contents where possible.
5. Select close-reading passages for each period before drafting prose claims.
